data.title: Arcturus Therapeutics: Two Catalysts, One Runway, And A Stock Priced For Failure
data.description:
I wrote about Arcturus Therapeutics (ARCT) before. At the time, the company was mostly seen as a Covid vaccine play. Well, the company is no longer a Covid story. Yes, the company still has exposure to its Covid assets, and the Kostaive program will help finance the rest of the portfolio, but the focus is now on two main asset therapeutics options: ARCT-032 (cystic fibrosis) and ARCT-810 (OTC deficiency).

We can boil down Arcturus as a “two shot biotech program” priced like a busted story, but with decent cash runway to keep swinging until 2028. I look at the Kostaive program more as a support asset that will generate some recurring cash going forward, since the company still holds 40% of the net profits of the program. Nevertheless, we should not assume the program alone will finance the company. Arcturus is best seen as an offset to dilution with a free call option on two interesting programs.

The reset

If you have followed my pieces on this stock, you will know that the company has done a wonderful job so far in protecting its balance sheet while spending the necessary cash to develop its therapy pipeline. The company did so through partnerships, especially in the Covid vaccine program, by adopting a payment schedule tied to R&D milestones. This has conserved cash runway without too much dilution (mainly SBC).

During the first half of 2025, the story for the company seemed solid, with the Kostaive program serving as a proof-of-concept and bringing in some cash, while retaining optionality on the rest of the pipeline. The troubles for the company emerged after reporting interim results for ARCT-032 (mRNA CF therapy), with the results casting doubts over the therapy's success.

Thesis Pillar #1: ARCT-032 (cystic fibrosis)

ARCT-032 is an inhaled therapy that delivers CFTR mRNA to the lungs. The interim phase II reports that the drug was “generally safe and well-tolerated” and found mucus reduction in 4 out of 6 patients. However, the October market reaction was violent because lung function (FEV1) did not show a meaningful improvement in the 28-day window of the trial. There’s still a bull unlock if the new 15 mg and 12-week study shows meaningful outcomes or if the FDA accepts the HRCT/mucus plug metrics as a surrogate endpoint, but that is a big assumption, and they might still need classic endpoints and longer studies, which means more time and burn.

The 12-week study is likely to be where the story graduates or stalls. This study is designed to provide longer exposure and more time for the patient physiology to reflect the structural gains from the therapy.

Thesis Pillar #2: ARCT-810 (OTC deficiency)

The OTC program is Arcturus's attempt to treat ornithine transcarbamylase deficiency by replacing the missing enzyme with an mRNA set of instructions, especially targeting the liver cells so that they can make their own OTC enzyme.

The interim phase 2 data was described as positive by the management team in the earnings call, and the company is preparing to engage with regulators in the first half of 2026 to align on the pivotal strategy. Given this is a rare disease and approvals depend mostly on biomarkers and well-accepted endpoints, this means the FDA alignment might be the real catalyst here. If the FDA agrees on an efficient design (smaller and faster), this can make the program a credible anchor asset for the company.

Financials

A quick glance at the financials shows us a modest cash burn coupled with a financial debt-free balance sheet and enough cash to go until 2028 (just like the management team reiterated in the last earnings call).

Revenue dropped mainly due to the Kostaive program moving to commercial status and the profit share of the program not being enough to offset the milestone payments in 3Q24. Nevertheless, the FCF improved versus the prior year, and liquidity seems adequate for the time being.

Valuation

The company is still in the early innings of developing its business. Therefore, the sales multiple is merely indicative and can even be misleading. The company still lacks significant and stable commercial revenues, and the market knows that.

Therefore, the valuation metrics are not necessarily telling us that the market considers Arcturus undervalued; they are mostly telling us that the company is a pipeline option. On the P/BV metric, we sense some market negativity. At 0.92 P/BV, the market is essentially telling us that it is heavily discounting the regulatory and future dilution risk in the current setup.

Risks

At this point, it is important to understand that the company is still burning cash, and that presents a big risk. Yes, the company has told us that the cash runway secures them until 2028, but that is a moving target; it assumes cost discipline and no major downside surprises (extended trials, etc…).

Most of the financial risks are associated with clinical and regulatory risks. The results from clinical trials carry several potential problems, and we would be naïve to consider a linear path from those trials to commercial launch. It is very seldom the case. In many instances, trial results will demand more trials than investors expect. Regulators might also stick with traditional endpoints, even if surrogate endpoints seem good.

One of the main positives for the company’s commercial operations, the Kostaive program, has been a drag lately, mostly due to the cash inflow from the partnership underwhelming investors. There is a big risk that the partner execution will keep missing prior expectations.

Wrapping-up

The company was performing well until the CF trial results disappointed investors. The Kostaive program’s performance in the flu season also ended up underwhelming investors, especially due to its inability to address the US market, limited penetration in Europe, and even lower than expected revenue in Japan.

All that depressed the stock price to a point where investors are paying a relatively modest value for a CF program with endpoint risk and an OTC program that has a decent shot at becoming the cleaner catalyst, if FDA alignment is favorable.

At this point we have two main re-rate triggers. First, the CF program data for the 15 mg and the 12-week trials might show outcomes that we care about (even if modestly), and second, the OTC program pivotal path that’s friendly for the company (manageable trial design). If at least one of these happens, I believe we might see a very significant re-rate.

In my opinion, the company is not a cheap value stock; I would rather see it as a busted biotech with real optionality. The below 1 P/BV multiple suggests market skepticism, and the low P/S suggests the market does not see the current revenue as enduring. This negative combo can be powerful on the upside if upcoming catalysts change the story. Moving from an “option” to a “program with a path,” in my opinion, is enough to change the multiples fast.

data.title: Uranium And Junior Mining Stocks With The URNJ ETF
data.description:
In the world of commodities, producers tend to do best during bull markets and suffer when the prices of the ores they extract from the Earth’s crust decline. While uranium does not actively trade on futures markets in the United States or Europe, it is becoming as critical a metal as the nonferrous metals traded on the London Metals Exchange and the precious metals traded on the CME’s COMEX and NYMEX divisions.

Most metal prices have moved significantly higher over the past few years, and uranium (UXA:COM) is no exception. The Sprott Junior Uranium Miners ETF (URNJ) could have substantial upside potential, as technical and fundamental factors remain bullish in February 2026.

Uranium is an Energy Commodity

Uranium is primarily an energy commodity, used to power nuclear power plants. However, it is a multipurpose commodity: it is a critical ingredient in nuclear reactors that power naval propulsion and the primary material for the cores of nuclear warheads. Uranium’s extreme density, approximately 70% higher than lead’s, makes depleted uranium ideal for use in armor-piercing projectiles and protective tank plating.

AI Infrastructure and Military Spending Requirements Are Bullish

The quest for green energy and growing demand for AI power are driving up nuclear power demand. Moreover, the bifurcation of the world’s nuclear powers, Russia’s war against Ukraine, NATO’s military buildup, China’s plans for reunification with Taiwan, Iran’s quest for enriched uranium for nuclear weapons, and increasing U.S. military spending are a potent combination of factors that are increasing the demand for uranium production.

Cameco is a Leading Uranium Producer - Its Share and Uranium Prices Are Trending Higher

The world’s leading uranium producers are:

While Kazakhstan leads the world in uranium production, Canada ranks second, with nearly twice Namibia's production in 2014. The top uranium-producing companies by market capitalization are:

Canadian producer Cameco (CCJ) is the leading publicly traded uranium-producing company.

Cameco publishes monthly uranium prices.

The chart shows that uranium prices reached a record high in 2007, then corrected and reached a low in 2016. Cameco’s spot and long-term uranium prices have been trending higher over the past ten years. The spot price was over $94 at the end of January 2026, with the long-term price at $89 per pound.

Junior Mining Stocks Tend to Outperform the Senior Mining Stocks on the Upside

The top five uranium producers by market capitalization had values above $10 billion. Meanwhile, the smaller uranium companies that explore for reserves and begin production on a much smaller basis tend to deliver higher returns on a percentage basis when uranium’s price rallies.

The daily chart shows that CCJ shares rallied 74% from $77.70 on November 21, 2025, to the latest high of $135.24 per share on January 29, 2026.

Over the same period, the Sprott Junior Uranium Miners ETF (URNJ) rallied 83% from $22.30 to $40.81 per share. The junior uranium-mining companies ETF outperformed CCJ, the miner with the largest market cap, on a percentage basis.

The Sprott Junior Uranium Miners ETF: A Bullish Trend is Our Best Friend as Fundamentals and Technicals Align

The fund summary for URNJ states:

URNJ’s most recent top holdings include:

At $33.19 per share, URNJ had $458.47 million in assets under management. URNJ trades an average of nearly 795,000 shares per day, making it a liquid ETF. URNJ charges a 0.80 management fee, and its latest annual dividend of $1.66 per share translates to a 5% yield, which covered the expense ratio in under one quarter in 2024.

Seeking Alpha’s ETF Grades for URNJ earn the highest possible A+ in the momentum category. The assets and trading volume receive a B+ for liquidity, while the dividends earn a C, possibly due to the potential for lower yield and payouts in 2025. The 0.80% management fee receives a D grade in the expense category, and URNJ’s only failing grade is in the risk category, due to the high volatility of uranium prices. Meanwhile, Seeking Alpha’s Quant Rating for URNJ is 22 out of 488 in the asset class, placing it in the top 5%.

The prospects for uranium demand over the coming months and years favor higher prices. Junior mining companies that explore for the ore and begin production tend to outperform the senior miners on a percentage basis. The technical trend and fundamental supply-and-demand picture are aligned; therefore, I rate URNJ a Buy in February 2026.

data.title: Neurocrine Biosciences: Expanding Opportunities, 2026 Will Be Less Exciting
data.description:
Steady execution on sales and clinical development builds value over time for biotechs but tends to be rather boring from a market sentiment standpoint. Neurocrine Biosciences (NBIX) is doing the right things to build value for its shareholders, but 2026 is looking pretty light on sentiment-changing data, and the company is going to have to improve its sales execution to drive upside.

Neurocrine shares are relatively flat since my last update, underperforming mid-teens appreciation for the biotech sector. Over the past year, the performances are closer, but Neurocrine has underperformed the sector by around 5%.

I believe Neurocrine is worth $181.50 per share, but sales execution with Ingrezza and Crenessity has to improve, and I expect the Street to remain concerned about pricing pressures on Ingrezza in the near term. I also see meaningful value in the pipeline, but programs in major depressive disorder and schizophrenia are high-risk, and the company won’t have much meaningful clinical data until 2027. All of that makes for a risk of a value trap where the lack of sentiment-changing data outside of earnings reports leads the shares to drift before a catalyst-rich 2027.

A Decent Fourth Quarter

Neurocrine’s fourth quarter results were fine, but not anything that will likely change the tone on the stock (guidance is another story, and I’ll get to that soon).

Revenue rose 28% year over year, beating by more than 1%, with product revenue up 29%. Ingrezza revenue rose 7% yoy and fell 4% qoq, matching sell-side expectations. The company’s newer drug, Crenessity, saw almost 38% sequential growth, beating by 12%.

Gross margin remains ridiculously good (97.8% vs. 98.5% a year ago). R&D spending was in line with expectations (up 42% yoy), while SG&A spending was about 3% higher (up 6% yoy). Operating income (non-GAAP) rose 30% but did miss by about 6%.

Neurocrine ended the quarter with $2.5 billion in cash and cash equivalents.

Guidance Didn’t Thrill

Neurocrine’s guidance relative to published expectations going into the quarter was fine, but not enough to beat the “whisper numbers” and excite the buy-side.

Management guided to $2.7 billion to $2.8 billion in Ingrezza sales for 2026, basically in line with the $2.73 billion sell-side number going in and representing about 9%-10% growth at the midpoint. Management is looking for mid-teens volume growth in a market still growing double digits but is expecting mid-single-digit pricing pressure.

Management did not provide specific guidance for Crenessity but did disclose that patient start forms declined to 431 from 540 in the prior quarter, and two-thirds of prescribing physicians have only written one script so far.

I’m not overly concerned about this. First, it’s not unusual for new drugs to hit an “air pocket” a few quarters after launch as the “early adopter” type physicians get on board quickly, but there isn’t a similar follow-through with the more rank-and-file prescribers. It’s also worth noting that CAH is a rare disease, and many physicians will only treat a small number of patients at a given time. Last but not least, many physicians effectively run their own ersatz trials, initially prescribing only for seriously ill patients or those that seem like ideal patients, and then seeing how it goes before broadening their prescribing. With that, I’m not worried about Crenessity’s blockbuster potential.

I would also note that Neurocrine guided to higher R&D (7% higher than expected) and SG&A (13% higher than expected). I believe the higher SG&A commitment is a good strategic move, but Neurocrine is in that weird space for biotechs where the Street is starting to care more about profits and cash flow than just revenue growth.

A Deepening Pipeline, But It Will Take Time To Mature

Neurocrine’s December R&D Day showcased an increasingly deep and broad pipeline as the company reinvests in new compounds and expanded product/target categories. The company has historically focused on small molecule drugs but will increasingly explore peptides, antibodies, gene therapies, and other modalities as circumstances warrant. This is likely to carry higher R&D costs and lower gross margins in the future, but in return, it significantly expands the range of addressable targets.

The most promising drugs in the pipeline today are arguably osavampator and direclidine.

Osavampator is a potential first-in-class AMPA-positive allosteric modulator for major depressive disorder (or MDD). Management is underway with three short-term acute Phase III studies, but top-line data won’t be available until 2027, and there will be additional read-outs in 2028 (randomized withdrawal) and 2029 (3-year open-label safety). Phase II data were promising, and management thinks the MDD category could be worth as much as $100 billion, given that Johnson & Johnson’s (JNJ) Spravato has approached $2 billion in revenue on less than 2% estimated penetration.

Direclidine is an M4 muscarinic agonist in Phase III development for schizophrenia and Phase II for bipolar mania. Top-line schizophrenia data should be available in 2027 and bipolar mania data in 2028. As I discussed in my last article, I have some concerns about whether direclidine will maintain enough efficacy between Phase II and Phase III testing (where declines are common), as the use of M4-only agonists is unproven (Bristol-Myers’s (BMY) Cobenfy is a dual M1/M4 agonist).

Behind these is a deep pipeline of candidates targeting Alzheimer’s-associated psychosis (NBI-‘569), Alzheimer’s cognition/Lewy body dementia (NBI-‘567), movement disorders (NBI-‘890), obesity (NBIP-2118), and CAH (NBIP-‘1435), as well as other compounds for women’s health, Friedreich’s ataxia, and undisclosed immunology indications.

NBIP-‘1435 is arguably the most interesting, given the sheer size of the obesity market and the possibility of this CRF1 antagonist to offer a different efficacy profile than incretins, including more positive muscle mass retention and better tolerability. Management intends to start a Phase I study in the first half of this year, so seeing data in 2026 would be a stretch.

Investors may get a preview of sorts from a competitor. Corteria has a CRF2 agonist in a Phase I study of obesity associated with heart failure, and data are expected in the second half of 2026.

The Outlook

The biggest potential driver or catalyst in the near term is marketing more effectively against Teva’s (TEVA) Austedo in tardive dyskinesia. A recent presentation at the ACNP annual meeting highlighted that Ingrezza achieves substantially higher VMAT2 target occupancy, with Ingrezza hitting the 80% target threshold at its starting dose (and achieving 90%+ occupancy over multiple doses), while Austedo can struggle to get to 80% with maximum dosing.

The real-world significance of this is debatable; doctors don’t read scientific literature as often as many investors would like to believe, but they will often respond to information presented to them, so I think this is a marketable development. I think it could be particularly relevant in neurology settings, where there does tend to be more responsiveness to data and where Teva enjoys a stronger share.

My expectations aren’t changing much relative to my prior modeling assumptions, as I had higher expectations for Crenessity than the Street. My valuation moves up about $2/share to $181.50/share, with Ingrezza driving more than $99/share of that (assuming over $3 billion in peak sales). Crenessity adds another $40/share, while osavampator and direclidine add about $22.50 and $13, respectively.

In both of those cases I’m using lower probabilities of success than I normally would for Phase III programs, as MDD and schizophrenia are notoriously difficult conditions (both have below-average success rates), and I have some qualms about direclidine’s Phase II performance.

The collected early-stage pipeline adds about $5/share, but there is significant upside here if and when future studies argue for higher probabilities of success for specific compounds (like NBIP-‘1435).

The Bottom Line

If Neurocrine can effectively market that recent target occupancy data on Ingrezza, I do see some upside to near-term sales expectations. Barring that, I do worry that a lack of meaningful clinical data could lead to Neurocrine falling off of investors’ priority lists and drifting until pivotal data in 2027. I believe that near-term value-trap risk is real, but I still see enough core value here to remain positive.

data.title: Johnson Controls Q1: Data Center Tailwinds Are Still In Place (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Introduction

Johnson Controls International plc (JCI) recently reported decent Q1 ’26 numbers with amazing growth in orders, which blew past estimates, leading to a decent share price increase since the report. I wanted to take a look at the numbers in more detail and give some comments on the outlook and why my stance on the company has somewhat changed since the last time I covered it back in January. I then said to hold onto your position, as data center tailwinds will help its share price in the future. Since then, the stock has been up 25%.

Now, since the company’s orders grew so much, I believe we will see a decent multiple contraction due to increased profitability, so I view JCI as a Buy going forward and still a decent diversified play once the AI data center hype passes.

By the Numbers

First quarter sales came in at around $5.8B, which were up 6.8% y/y, and beat estimates by $160m. The American segment increased 6% to $3.84B, EMEA 9% to $1.26B, and APAC 8% to $693m. So, not that impressive; however, what is a lot more impressive is that the company’s Q1 orders increased 39% y/y organically. Now the backlog stands at around $18.2B, which increased 20%.

This impressive increase was fueled by the need for advanced cooling solutions in data centers. I don’t think that was a surprise at all, considering how much the hyperscalers are looking to spend going forward on their AI dreams over the next few years. Americas saw orders surge 56% y/y, heavily weighted toward applied HVAC and Controls for data centers.

Let’s take a look at the company’s profitability this past quarter. The American segment saw its gross margin increase 6% to $1.375B, which translates to close to 36% in GM. Adjusted EBITA margin is around 16.4%, up 20bps y/y. The EMEA gross margins improved 13% to $448m, giving us a 35.5% margin, while adjusted EBITA improved 21% to $164m, giving us a 13% margin, which is up 120bps y/y. APAC showed the best performance in terms of percentages, as its gross margin improved 6% to $251m, giving us a 38.4% margin and an adjusted EBITA margin of 16.9%, a 290bps improvement y/y.

Looking at the company’s financial position, JCI finished the first quarter with around $552m in cash and equivalents, against $8.7B in long-term debt. The debt pile remains significant, however, far from risky, given how well the company is performing. The company’s interest coverage expense stood at around 12x, which is a lot higher than what analysts consider to be healthy, which is 2x. If that is not enough, the company also increased its cash from operations by 145%, coming in at $611m, which makes its free cash flow for the quarter around $531m compared to last year’s first quarter of $133m. That is a massive improvement; however, the company’s adjusted free cash flow dipped to $428m from $603m last year. It's all mostly due to timing issues, so I wouldn’t worry about the 30% drop.

Overall, the company is a lot more profitable, is able to collect better from customers, and manages inventory levels very well, even if there is high demand. The management’s working capital management is commendable.

The management also provided us with some color on the next quarter, as well as for the full-year 2026. For the next quarter, JCI expects organic sales growth to grow by around 5%, while analysts expected before the report around 4%-4.5%. For EPS, the company now expects to make $1.11, while the consensus expected it to be $1.05, so that was a beat on both the top and bottom line in the guidance. In terms of the full-year 2026, the management even raised its guidance for EPS while keeping sales unchanged. Sales are expected to grow mid-single digits, while adjusted EPS was increased to around $4.70 from the previously guided $4.55. After the raise, the guidance came in ahead of consensus also, by around 8 cents.

So, overall, I think the report was very positive for the company. The massive improvements in the order intake most likely overshadowed any other metric mentioned here. On the day, the company’s share price surged over 6% and has increased ever since, continuing to reach new 52-week highs. It was a reasonable reaction to the company’s order intake. Nothing has been completely blown out of proportion. I was actually surprised that we saw only around a 6% increase on the day, given how much the company’s backlog increased in the Americas and overall. But I prefer seeing such a reaction as opposed to the one that moves the company’s share price by 25%, which is mostly unsustainable and risks coming back down as people take profit. This is a lot more sustainable.

Comments on the Outlook

With most, if not all, hyperscalers already reporting their numbers and given their capex budget forecast for 2026, we can see that they are not taking their foot off the accelerator any time soon. The AI hypergrowth story is alive and well for at least another year or three, based on hyperscalers' hundreds of billions in AI capex to be spent over the next while, as well as tremendous bottlenecks in the high bandwidth memory (“HBM”) space from the likes of Micron (MU), Western Digital (WDC), and SanDisk (SNDK), all of which already have filled up their capacity through at least 2027.

While they are building more capacity, it will take at least two years to see any increases here. JCI will continue to benefit from this AI revolution going forward, and as the new liquid-cooling systems make up a higher percentage of the overall sales in the future, the company’s profitability will continue to increase, leading to quite a decent multiples contraction.

The company has guided for not only margin sustainability but also improvement as the company continues to scale aggressively. The management guided for at least 50% operating leverage for the full year, which means the company’s profitability is going to continue to improve. Analysts are projecting very healthy EPS improvement over time, which I think is rather conservative still, given how much demand there is for the company’s data center products. For example, here we can see that the company expects to see around 5% in sales growth for 2026, while an increase in EPS of 25%. So, 50% leverage means that on every $100m in sales, half of it goes straight into EBIT.

I think EPS growth is going to be around 25% for at least two years and then start to go down to a more sustainable range with time. To around 15%, I would say. And if we see sales numbers grow at a much faster rate than the 5%-6% guided, then the EPS growth could be sustainable for a lot longer.

I am a little worried about what is going to happen after these massive years of growth, fueled by the insatiable hunger of the hyperscalers in the AI race. It is not sustainable, and there will be a day when they are going to say they built up all they needed and are putting the brakes on. Then all of these companies that were involved in any shape or form within the AI data center infrastructure will see orders evaporate and profitability crater.

JCI will feel some pain once that happens, but luckily, the company is not a one-trick pony. As the order growth dominated the headlines recently, we can see that the data center revenues accounted for around 15% of total revenues. The company’s operations have been around for well over 100 years, and in that time, JCI has built up a massive, diversified portfolio of Healthcare & Life Sciences, Higher Education institutions, and Government. The company’s service revenues will continue to provide decent sales in the future, and if the money for new data centers stops tomorrow, they will still collect billions in high-margin checks to keep the current data centers running.

Should you invest in JCI?

This will all depend on how exposed you are to the AI growth story of the past couple of years. Over the past few months, I’ve been looking to steer away from AI plays by buying some companies that have essentially nothing to do with AI and data centers, and so far, I look like a fool, as all of the AI/data center plays continue to skyrocket in the portfolio, while the diversified companies are lagging the performance quite a bit.

However, JCI may be a bit different, since it is not a pure AI/data center play. Sure, it may look like that right now since that is the highest growth driver right now, but once the money evaporates, the company’s other business segments will start to pick up the AI/data center slack, so I am considering JCI to be a decent diversified play that continues to take advantage of the AI wave and also has some built-in protection for when that growth story is no more. Therefore, I am upgrading it to a Buy.

data.title: abrdn Emerging Markets Fund Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Fund performance

The Fund rose by 4.59% over the quarter, lagging the benchmark slightly by 18 basis points. Some performance was given back in December due to weakness in China, but we ended the year in positive territory, thanks to a strong turnaround in the second half that saw the portfolio outperform by over 300 basis points. 1

In Taiwan, the AI-driven tech rally continued, lifting local tech stocks that are at the epicentre of the US AI infrastructure buildout. Stock selection was positive. ASE Technology shares rose as the company increased capacity for its advanced packaging and testing and won new orders by tapping into rising AI-driven demand. Index heavyweight Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Co was another contributor to performance.

South Korea also did well. The market and our holdings there are beneficiaries of both the US AI infrastructure build as well as the local "Value-up" programme – a government-led initiative to push Korean corporates to improve shareholder returns. AI plays such as memory chip producers SK Hynix and Samsung Electronics outperformed, alongside power equipment maker Hyundai Electric. 'Value-up' play Samsung C&T also delivered strong returns.

In Indonesia, easing concerns around the government and its policies saw investors return to the market, which boosted our holdings, Bank Negara Indonesia and Telkom Indonesia. Telkom was further helped by increased clarity around its efforts to refocus the business, boost profitability and return cash to shareholders.

Elsewhere, copper miner Grupo Mexico rallied on rising copper demand, driven by electrification and data centre growth. The miner, with its access to low-cost reserves, remains a long-term beneficiary.

China and Hong Kong were detractors over the quarter. Markets sold off as investors took profits amid softer growth in China and the underwhelming government response. The Fund's overall overweight exposure hurt performance, as did our preference for consumer-related stocks like Alibaba and Tencent. Both names were hit hard despite their robust prospects. Weaker consumption and increased competition further affected Giant Biogene, a synthetic collagen producer, though the company announced the successful licensing of its medical aesthetics product in China, with promising prospects.

In the United Arab Emirates, our holdings in Abu Dhabi Islamic Bank and Aldar Properties trailed as investors rotated out of winners into laggards. Both companies continue to report good results and remain upbeat about prospects in 2026.

Market review

Emerging market equities ended the quarter higher and outperformed developed markets, led by a near 28% jump in South Korea. China, however, lagged relative to peers.

Technology stocks rallied, driven by semiconductors, specifically memory stocks, as confirmed chip shortages resulted in noticeable price increases for DRAM (dynamic random-access memory) and HBM (high-bandwidth memory) chips. This proved particularly beneficial for South Korean heavyweights and leading memory chip producers.

In the US, following interest rate cuts in September and October, the Federal Reserve reduced rates again in December and signalled further easing in 2026. While economic data remains mixed, growing concerns over the US labour market saw the central bank move more in line with President Donald Trump's desire for lower rates. The market's response was positive as the easing cycle has come without a recession, which bodes well for emerging market risk assets.

Commodities such as gold, silver and copper experienced strong demand as hedges against policy risk and for industrial use, which benefitted resource-heavy Latin American markets and South Africa. Geopolitical tensions, policy uncertainty as well as US monetary policy easing have increased demand for gold and silver, while copper and other minerals have gained from artificial intelligence (AI) infrastructure development, electric vehicle adoption and energy transition.

Activity

We added Brazilian real estate developer Multiplan Empreendimentos Imobiliarios (MLTTY), Kazakhstan uranium producer NAC Kazatomprom (NATKY) and global miner Rio Tinto (RIO) over the quarter, in view of their attractive prospects.

Conversely, we sold ICICI Bank (IBN), MercadoLibre (MELI) and Shenzhen Envicool Technology to fund better opportunities elsewhere.

Outlook

Markets remain centred on hopes around the future of AI. This is despite continuing geopolitical risks linked to Trump's transactional use of the world's most powerful office and retreat from global security commitments, and rising risks of declining fiscal discipline in other developed markets.

AI delivery has become a critical component of the US economy. We expect Beijing to build a rival AI ecosystem, maintaining its focus on localisation and anti-involvement. Despite deflation concerns, southbound flows have accelerated in 2025, extending beyond 'quasi-fixed income' names which were seen as high-yielding alternatives to bonds amid China's low interest rates. This may suggest greater risk appetite among Chinese investors and could drive a wealth effect that supports consumption if the stock market continues to outperform.

Overall, Trump's tariffs suggest that his goals of shifting manufacturing and raising revenue for tax cuts are prime and centre. Market consensus continues to see a move towards a breakdown in China-US trade, though the pace and extent of decoupling remain a key uncertainty. We believe China will continue to use its leverage to arrive at some accord. Trump has also extended tariff pressure to India and Brazil, but we expect an agreement to reduce tariffs will be reached. However, the timing is hard to predict given domestic politics and populist leaders.

We remain constructive on the outlook for emerging markets, supported by a weaker US dollar, lower rates and broad capex themes such as AI, electrification and changing supply chains. Valuations remain relatively attractive, though we are mindful of an emerging AI bubble and broader risks. Our focus on quality remains key.

data.title: Unity: The Market Is Right, This Is Still A Sell
data.description:
A few weeks ago, Alphabet (GOOGL) (GOOG) showed an AI model capable of generating games with just a few prompts, and this made the market more uncertain about some gaming stocks, such as studios like Take-Two Interactive (TTWO), Nintendo (NTDOY), and Unity Software Inc. (U). I disagree with the market in part. I don't think Alphabet's AI will be a major threat to gaming studios, but I do think it highlights the competitive landscape for middlemen in a few years, which is the case with Unity.

So even with the market's drastic reaction, which caused U stock to fall 25% after Q4 earnings, I agree, and I still think it's not a good stock to have in your portfolio, not only because of competitive threats but also because of its unpleasant financials. Therefore, I maintain a sell rating for Unity stock, and even with all this market pessimism, I still think a substantial bounce in U stock is a “big if.”

Unity's Q4 Earnings: Not That Bad

Looking at Unity's Q4 earnings in isolation, it wasn't that bad. Revenue grew 10%, driven by Grow Solutions, especially the good growth of Unity Vector, which is now one of the main pillars of the case because it's related to both AI and advertising. And comparing the top line with the company's previous guidance, it not only met but exceeded it.

Compared to what the market expected, it's also in line; in fact, there was a beat of $10 million on the top line and $0.03 on EPS. But one of the problems was Q1 guidance. The company indicated net sales between $480 million and $490 million, while the market expected something closer to $492 million. It's not a huge difference, but it does raise some questions.

If we compare it to what the market expected for Q4, we're talking about $492.8 million in sales, and for Q1, $491.7 million, which is stable QoQ. But even though the company beat estimates, it indicated lower revenue for the next quarter, indicating a slight downward trend QoQ. Nothing too worrying, but given the context and valuation, I don't think it's good.

In terms of EBITDA margin, it was also a solid quarter, with the margin increasing by a few percentage points, with adjusted EBITDA increasing 17% YoY, above the pace of revenue.

In addition, operations are not showing any negative signs, with the CEO mentioning that Unity 6 is being adopted more than any version in the history of Unity. So, in short, it seems to be another solid quarter, but I will highlight why I still think it is a sell.

I Have No Conviction In Unity For The Next 20 Years

One of the main points when talking about valuation is conviction. In order to start making growth assumptions and then do some valuation calculations, you first need to believe that the business model is sustainable, that there are good avenues for growth, and that management will be able to execute in a healthy manner and at a pace that's minimally attractive in relation to the company's market cap.

Well, that's not quite the case I see for Unity. Aside from the old problems of operational surprises, which, in my view, is a negative point for Unity’s management, which executed some initiatives poorly and had to backtrack, I still see other problems. The problem is not with Unity's engine itself, which is currently one of the leading engines on the market, and even with the growth of Epic Games' Unreal Engine, it's not performing poorly or anything like that.

The problem is when I think about the next 10 or 20 years. With Alphabet's new AI, it was possible to get a glimpse of what we can expect in the coming years, and particularly, I think the middleman is very squeezed in this scenario.

I think companies like Take-Two Interactive, which owns IPs like Grand Theft Auto, may even benefit, as I don't think Alphabet will start creating games or that so many games will emerge that they would unseat the world's largest studios. Making a good game isn't just about prompts or a rigid process; there are many intangible things involved, such as a creative process, narrative, and often a well-built franchise. And with more advanced AI-driven engines, I think studios like Rockstar Games will be able to make better games faster and at a lower cost. This also implies greater competition for companies like Unity or Unreal.

Rockstar is a good example. It's one of the largest in this industry and does not use either of these engines. Instead, it uses its own engine. I think this is the way forward for large studios: Developing something in-house or hiring a service such as Alphabet to scale up their creative production. I mean, more proprietary, customized engines and fewer uses of "standard" engines.

For small developers, there's still a certain barrier to entry. Many developers have already learned how to use Unity/Unreal, and many things are already designed to integrate with this engine and/or have greater compatibility. At the same time, I think AI helps to remove this barrier to entry and increases competition, especially in the long run.

Maybe Unity will actually grow as the market expects (~14%) in 2026 and 2027, but what about 2030? Can you be sure that some company won't steal part of that market share or that some new uncertainty won't loom over Unity and make the prospects worse? Personally, I can't be very confident about that, even with initiatives like Unity Vector.

Unity's Financials Are Worse Than They Seem

The icing on the cake for my sell rating on Unity is the financials. On the surface they look good enough, with double-digit revenue growth and margin gains, but under GAAP, Unity is still delivering a net loss. Mainly because in non-GAAP metrics, Unity removes stock-based compensation, which is a non-cash expense, but reduces shareholder value created because it dilutes shareholders.

In Q4 2024, the net loss was -$122 million, and in Q4 2025 it was -$89 million. It's improving, but it's still far from being a healthy company in this sense. Even if we look only at operating income, it's actually an operating loss, which was -$106 million in the quarter. I understand that depreciation is high and that this does not really affect cash flow, but stock-based compensation is also really high, at $92 million in the period, so even though Unity managed to generate ~$120 million in cash flow in the quarter, the shareholder value created was much lower than that.

Another point is that even if we use operating cash flow as a value creation metric, Unity is not that attractive. We're talking about something close to $480 million per year. But even after this drop, it's trading for more than $9 billion, which gives an implied multiple close to 20x, and remember, this is disregarding stock-based compensation.

And that, combined with all the uncertainties surrounding the company and the industry, I don't think it's an asymmetric move; in fact, I think it's a very risky move.

The Bottom Line

I didn't think Unity's Q4 earnings were bad, but I'm still not optimistic enough to see Unity as an attractive stock for a long-term portfolio. There are still many uncertainties, and the case still seems like a non-asymmetric bet, i.e., in a base scenario, you get a reasonable shareholder yield, but in a stressed scenario, the company may experience de-rating, a drop in revenue caused by competition, and so on.

Therefore, my rating remains a sell for Unity.

data.title: Upstream Bio: Hold Rating As Verekitug Faces Competition Among Latest Asthma Data
data.description:
The last time I spoke about Upstream Bio, Inc. (UPB), it was in a Seeking Alpha article entitled "Upstream Bio: Maintaining Buy Rating On Verekitug Success In Phase 2 CRSwNP Study." With respect to this article, I noted that the company was able to achieve highly competitive data for its thymic stromal lymphopoietin [TSLP] inhibitor Verekitug for the treatment of patients with chronic rhinosinusitis with nasal polyps [CRSwNP]. This was in terms of once every 12 weeks [Q12W] dosing that offered competitive differentiation. Based on this, I had placed a Buy rating on this stock because of it.

Today, I'm downgrading UPB stock because the hope was that the company could offer competitive dosing for another program that it just released data for. This would be in terms of it releasing positive top-line results from its phase 2 VALIANT study using several different dosing regimens of Verekitug to treat these severe asthma patients.

Both the 100 mg Q12W and 400 mg once every 24 weeks [Q24W] doses achieved a statistically significant outcome compared to placebo in terms of the primary endpoint of annualized asthma exacerbation rate [AAER] in patients with severe asthma. If this is the case, then why do I believe the stock should be downgraded to a Hold rating? That's because while the company has plans to move forward towards pivotal phase 3 studies of Verekitug to treat both patients with severe asthma and CRSwNP, with respect to the severe asthma program, the goal was not to be equivalent to Tezspire. The advantage is that once every 3 months, dosing could achieve superior efficacy and be competitive. In addition, there is the hope that the Q24W data released would end up also exceeding the bar set by Tezspire in terms of AAER and other potential drugs in development.

As I will explain below, the problem is that there are several other companies that are in the process of developing TSLP-targeting drugs for such indications as well. They are exploring once every 6 months [Q24W] dosing as well. If they end up offering superior efficacy, then that could end up putting Upstream Bio's Verkitug program in targeting severe asthma and CRSwNP in dire straits. With the phase 2 VALIANT study offering up competitive once-every-3-months dosing of Verekitug for the treatment of patients with severe asthma, plus the fact that there are several competitors that could potentially offer superior efficacy in the very same patient population, I believe that Upstream Bio's stock should be downgraded for now to a Hold rating.

Verekitug for Severe Asthma Wins, but Future Advancement Hinges on Competition

As I noted above, Upstream Bio reported positive results from its phase 2 VALIANT study using its TSLP inhibitor Verekitug to treat patients with severe asthma. Before going over the clinical data released from this mid-stage study, plus any catalysts to come about from this program, I believe that it is first important to understand what this disorder is and what the possible market opportunity for the company could end up being.

Asthma is characterized as a chronic, long-term, uncurable disease whereby inflammation and narrowing of the air passageways occur. The thing to note about this disorder is that it also causes an overproduction of mucus as well. Why are all these factors bad? That's because all of them lead to the main problem associated with Asthma, which is the inability to breathe effectively. Some symptoms that these patients can experience are as follows:

  • Constant coughing
  • Shortness of breath
  • Pain in the chest
  • Wheezing.

The attempt for patients is to avoid triggers that cause this disorder, but that can't always be helped. Especially since another factor that causes it would be genetic. The global asthma therapeutics market size is projected to reach $37.91 billion by 2030. This is a very large market opportunity, except there is something of importance to mention here with this specific pool of patients. The company is not going after the entire Asthma patient population but instead only going after severe Asthma patients. Such patients have symptoms that can't be dealt with even if they are given high-dose corticosteroids and other long-acting therapies.

Where is the opportunity here? Well, the problem lies in the fact that despite biologics being available, not many patients are on them. In the United States there are 1.3 million Biologic-eligible severe Asthma patients that the company can go after with Verekitug. Consider that globally there are about 350 million people worldwide with Asthma, and from this, between about 5% and 10% are considered to have severe Asthma. The main point I'm getting at here, which I will be showing directly below in the clinical data, is that the company is attempting to help such inflammatory patients with less frequent dosing and/or injections.

In order to find out if Verekitug would be able to help treat these patients with severe asthma, it ran the phase 2 VALIANT study. This mid-stage trial recruited a total of 478 patients who were treated for a minimum of 24 weeks and a maximum of 60 weeks. Such patients were given a subcutaneous dose for the VALIANT study as follows:

  • 100 mg of Verekitug once every 24 weeks [Q24W]
  • 400 mg of Verekitug Q24W
  • 100 mg of Verekitug Q12W.

The primary endpoint of this study was set to be that of the Annual Asthma Exacerbation Rate [AAER] at week 60. The trial had met the primary endpoint in that there was a meaningful reduction in AAER of 56% for patients dosed at 100 mg Q12W and then 39% for patients dosed at 400 mg Q24W. When being compared to Tezspire, this is very good data. That's because the company matches closely with efficacy but offers a more convenient dosing schedule [less frequent dosing].

The reason for the stock price declining after the release of data has to do with several competitors who are also targeting the TSLP pathway with extended dosing. They are also attempting 6-month dosing, and if they can clear the bar for efficacy to be better than Verekitug, then that would put into question whether or not Upstream Bio can be competitive against them.

Many such companies are still working on their respective candidates, which is why I have placed a Hold rating on Upstream for now. It just honestly depends on what type of efficacy they clear when they release newly updated data. Generate Biomedicines is in the process of evaluating GB-0895 as an adjunctive therapy in adults and adolescents with severe uncontrolled Asthma in the ongoing phase 3 SOLAIRIA2 study. The point here is that this drug is to be given to patients via subcutaneous [SC] injection once every 6 months. GB-0895 is a long-acting anti-TSLP antibody generated using AI. GSK, aka GlaxoSmithKline (GSK), is in the process of running a 6-month treatment regimen of the long-lasting TSLP drug GSK5784283 in a phase 2 study. This mid-stage trial is also recruiting patients with uncontrolled asthma.

Lastly, there is a private biotech by the name of Winward Bio, which initiated its phase 2 POLARIS study using long-acting, anti-TSLP monoclonal antibody WIN378. It won't be long for data to come out from this program, though, because Windward believes that it could release data from this study in mid-2026.

As far as some catalysts for Upstream Bio, the goal for the company is to release full detailed results from the phase 2 VALIANT study using Verekitug for the treatment of patients with severe asthma at an upcoming medical conference in 2026. In addition, there is an expectation that the company will meet with the FDA to discuss plans for being able to initiate phase 3 studies of Verekitug to treat both patients with severe asthma and also patients with CRSwNP as well.

Financials

According to the 10-Q SEC Filing, Upstream Bio had cash, cash equivalents, and short-term investments of $372.4 million as of September 30, 2025. The reason for the cash on hand is because to date there have been two major transactions to bring in a lot of cash. One transaction was the receipt of $400 million of gross proceeds from the issuance and sale of its redeemable convertible preferred stock. Plus, the approximately $268.8 million of net proceeds generated from its IPO as well.

I believe that this company is well capitalized, and this is not based on my own estimate. This is something that the company notes in its Q3 of 2025 financial report, which is that it believes that its cash on hand will be enough to fund its operations through 2027. The company burns roughly $38.5 million of cash per quarter. This is broken down into $33 million in R&D expenses and then $5.5 million in G&A expenses.

Risks to Business

There are several risks that investors should be aware of before investing in Upstream Bio. The first risk to consider would be in terms of the positive data that the company just obtained from the phase 2 VALIANT study using its drug Verekitug for the treatment of patients with severe asthma. The company intends to meet with the FDA, and the hope is that it will be able to discuss the ability to begin phase 3 studies for both the severe Asthma and CRSwNP programs.

The risk here is that there is no guarantee that the FDA is going to greenlight or allow this company to begin a late-stage study for either of these programs based on the data I just went over above.

The second risk to consider would be in terms of Upstream Bio's actual data from the study. As I went over above, the company produced a competitive reduction of AAER of 56% in patients dosed at 100 mg Q12W and then 39% for patients dosed at 400 mg Q24W. This data holds well against Tezspire, especially since it is dosed once every 4 weeks. The problem lies with the competitors I went over above. Each of them is in the process of developing their own long-acting anti-TSLP antibody to treat patients with severe Asthma.

The risk here is that if any of these companies produces efficacy that far exceeds what Upstream Bio has been able to accomplish thus far, especially with Q24W dosing, then the biotech will have a major problem on its hands.

The third and final risk to consider would be in terms of the company's other ongoing program, which is the evaluation of Verekitug being explored in the phase 2 VENTURE study for the treatment of patients with Chronic Obstructive Pulmonary Disorder [COPD]. The first risk here is that there is no assurance that the final data to be released from this particular study program will end up being positive, nor that the primary endpoint is going to be met in a statistically significant manner. Secondly, the design of this study was laid out in such a way that if certain efficacy endpoints are met, then Upstream Bio might be allowed to submit a regulatory application for approval.

The risk here is that even though such other efficacy endpoints were laid out, there is no assurance that the FDA is going to allow for approval based on these alone. It might be that the company might be forced to run a larger phase 3 study regardless of what endpoint is achieved in the ongoing phase 2 study.

Conclusion

I believe it is important to downgrade Upstream Bio's stock to a Hold rating. I think that achieving statistically significant outcomes from its phase 2 VALIANT study using Verekitug to treat patients with severe Asthma is ideal. However, it goes back to what I noted beforehand, which is that it's all up to competitors now. If GlaxoSmithKline, Generate Biomedicines, or Windward Bio do well in terms of achieving superior AAER with Q24W dosing, then that would be very bad news for Upstream Bio. For now it presses on, and there are catalysts for it to potentially boost shareholder value.

A thing to keep an eye on would be data releases from these other companies developing their own long-acting anti-TSLP antibodies for the treatment of patients with severe Asthma. The reason why is because if one of such companies boasts highly positive data, then it could have a negative impact on Upstream Bio. For all of the reasons I have gone over above, this is a wait-and-see type of investment. Thus, the reason why I believe that it is important to downgrade this stock to a Hold rating.

data.title: First Eagle Global Income Builder Fund Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market Overview

Risk assets in general capped off a strong year with a solid fourth quarter. In contrast with recent trends, non-US equity markets led the way in 2025.

While the threats to market stability that have prevailed throughout the year were undiminished during the quarter, investors continued to embrace risk. The S&P 500 Index gained 2.7% for the fourth quarter and 17.9% for the year, while the MSCI EAFE Index returned a respective 4.9% and 31.2%. Non-US fixed income also saw success in 2025, with the Bloomberg Global Aggregate Index returning 8.2% for the year while the Bloomberg US Bond Aggregate Index gained 7.3%. Notably, 2025 was only the third year in the past 10 that the MSCI EAFE has outperformed the S&P 500. Gold, meanwhile, surged 65% during the year, its largest annual return since 1979. 1

The US Double Bind Gets Tighter

Despite ample motivation for conservatism in an environment of pronounced macro, financial, geopolitical and structural concerns, risk perception in US markets remains low pretty much wherever you look. Equity market valuation multiples are rich, high yield spreads are tight and implied volatility is low. 2

While the post-pandemic normalization of certain macro factors has been encouraging, the country's fiscal settings remain far off-kilter. The US federal deficit remains historically outsized relative to the unemployment rate—as it has since the outbreak of Covid-19. 3 Normally, high unemployment rates and recession beget large fiscal deficits, as lower tax revenues combine with increased government spending. Conversely, low unemployment rates and robust economic growth typically support higher tax revenues and tighter fiscal policy, causing deficits to contract or even turn into surpluses. If the economy were in balance, we'd expect budget deficits of around 2% of GDP—not the 5.8% at the end of fiscal year 2025. 4

We believe this persistent deficit spending helps explain the decoupling of gold and Treasuries seen in recent years. The price of gold surged 65% during 2025—its largest annual gain since 1979—and has more than doubled over the past two years in an apparent acknowledgement of the double-bind facing US policymakers: Doing nothing to address the deficit could stoke renewed inflationary pressures, while taking action to curb it would likely increase the possibility of recession. More recent rallies in the prices of other precious metals like silver and platinum appear to reflect the same policy conundrum. 5

The US is not alone in this regard, of course, as fiscal deterioration has been widespread across both advanced and emerging economies. However, the US is among only a few key economies—alongside the UK and Brazil—facing twin deficits, and this combination of negative fiscal and current account balances represents an incremental risk that most others do not face. 6 The US current account deficit reflects an imbalance between savings and investment in the economy, which, by formula, must be offset by inflows of foreign capital into the US. The current account deficit is not necessarily a bad thing; the US has long been a popular destination for foreign investment, bolstered by the dollar's status as the global reserve currency. However, it does complicate efforts to consolidate fiscal policy.

Twin deficits are nothing new for the US, which has run them consistently since the early 1980s with only a few exceptions, the most recent being 2001. 7 More often than not, however, the fiscal deficit as a percentage of GDP has exceeded the current account deficit; efforts to bring the fiscal deficit to levels less than that of the current account deficit have the potential to bleed into the private sector, impacting free cash flow and causing corporate credit issues. 8

Beyond this fiscal reckoning is a question of how long the tailwinds that have supported both economic and equity market growth in recent years can persist. Chief among these has been the massive spending on the buildout of artificial intelligence (AI) infrastructure. Spending on semiconductor fabrication and data centers on average has accounted for 0.4% of GDP growth annually since 2022, and the growth of technology investment overall has contributed nearly half of GDP growth in recent quarters. 9

Capital expenditures (CAPEX) by hyperscalers—companies like Amazon (AMZN), Apple (AAPL), Meta (META), Microsoft (MSFT) and Oracle (ORCL) that operate massive data centers supporting cloud computing—have grown rapidly over the past decade or so, at a pace far exceeding that of cash flow from operations. 10 As a result, capex as a percentage of cash flow from operations for these companies has grown from about 20% in 2015 to 70% today, and what had been very free-cash-flow generative businesses are now decidedly less so. 11 Spending on data centers and other AI infrastructure by hyperscalers is forecast to continue, but its current rate of growth is unsustainable absent some other sources of financing. To us, it seems likely to decelerate toward the growth rate of operating cash flow for these companies, which may represent an unwelcome plot twist in the market's AI narrative.

Finding Ballast Across Assets

Already-high geopolitical tensions ratcheted up a notch in early 2026 when the US took military action on Venezuelan soil to remove President Nicolas Maduro. The Trump administration has publicly offered a range of justifications for forcing leadership change in Venezuela—including the illegitimacy of the elections that brought Maduro to power, the country's role in the international drug trade and the seizure of US oil interests, among others. To us, one clear, if unspoken, goal of the operation was to check the influence of China and Russia on Venezuela and Latin America in general. Both nations have close diplomatic and economic ties in the region and staunchly oppose US dominance.

While escalations such as we have seen in Latin America in recent weeks are largely unpredictable, they are not surprising amid a geopolitical order in flux. We've spoken in recent years about the emergence of the Eurasian heartland, with authoritarian powers concentrated in eastern Europe and Asia—China, Russia, Iran and North Korea—growing increasingly aligned. More recently, the behavior of the US, long seen as reliable partner to like-minded countries worldwide, has led many to question the durability of its traditional alliances. Geopolitics is a complex system, and the current disequilibrium has increased the possibility of destabilizing left-tail events—be they in the Americas, Europe or Asia.

Perhaps unsurprisingly in this uncertain environment, the monetary value of gold has been reasserting itself. Earlier, we noted the significant increase in the gold price over the past two years, but this rally has merely aligned gold with its 50-year geometric average relative to the stock of US public debt while bringing it closer to its geometric average versus the S&P 500. 12 And though we're attuned to the risk inherent in such a sharp price move, we continue to highly value its strategic hedge potential given the fiscal and geopolitical dynamics currently in place.

Gold, however, is not the only source of portfolio ballast. Nor is cash. In fact, one of our focuses in recent years has been on building portfolio resilience through equities we believe offer ballast through their lower risk character. This is not achieved simply through higher allocations to traditionally defensive segments of the market like health care and consumer staples, though we do have meaningful exposure to these sectors. Rather, we evaluate stocks across industries from the bottom up in search of attributes we believe contribute to low correlations to the broader market, including strong balance sheets, high margins, diverse product lineups, long-lived assets and contractually obligated revenues.

We take a similar bottom-up approach to the fixed income sleeve of the portfolio. US Treasury yields remain somewhat above neutral policy rates offering value across the monetary cycle. Meanwhile Treasury inflation-protected securities still price inflation in line with the Federal Reserve's target. In a fragmenting global environment with elevated debt, we find inflation protection desirable. In addition, our bias toward higher-quality, shorter-duration positions gives us opportunities to recycle capital when we see attractive credit investments emerge in a market where spreads remain very tight.

Last year demonstrated that high benchmark correlations are not a prerequisite for strong portfolio performance, and we believe our benchmark-agnostic approach will serve clients well in markets that may be less accommodating going forward.

Portfolio Review

Global Income Builder Fund A Shares (without sales charge*) posted a return of 5.13% in the fourth quarter 2025. As of December 31, 2025, the Fund's equity allocation was 63.37%, comprising 41.47% international stocks and 27.90% US stocks. The Fund's 22.06% bond allocation included 16.67% in investment grade issues. The Global Income Builder Fund outperformed the composite index in the period.

Leading contributors in the First Eagle Global Income Builder Fund this quarter included gold bullion, Samsung Electronics Co., Ltd. (SSNLF), and Power Corporation of Canada (PWCDF). Leading fixed income contributors included Mexico Remittances Funding Fiduciary Estate Management Sarl 12.5%, due 15-Oct-2031, Government of the United States of America 6.125%, due 15-Nov-2027, and Government of the United States Of America 6.625% due 15-Feb-2027.

As noted in the Market Commentary, gold bullion continued to rally in the quarter on the back of such drivers as geopolitical uncertainty, strong demand from central banks and exchange-traded funds (ETFs) and a dovish Federal Reserve.

Samsung Electronics is a global technology company and major manufacturer of diverse electronic components with a dominant presence in memory semiconductors. Results during the quarter reflect continued strong demand and shortage-induced pricing strength for DRAM chips and persistent demand from hyperscalers driven by AI infrastructure buildouts amid tight supply.

Power Corp. of Canada is a family-owned holding company with interests in a diversified lineup of insurance and asset management businesses in North America, Europe and Asia. Restructuring initiatives have taken hold; the company's subsidiaries are doing well and improved returns on equity reflect enhanced capital efficiency. With strong fundamentals and defensive characteristics, Power Corp.'s controlling family appears to demonstrate a thoughtful approach to risk management.

The Mexico remittances bonds referenced above are secured by a first lien on the remittance cash flow from the US to Mexico through the money-transfer business of Nueva Elektra del Milenio, the retail subsidiary of Mexican conglomerate Grupo Elektra. Although down slightly for the quarter—reflecting shifts in US immigration policy—remittance volumes remained robust and were bolstered by the steeper yield curve and currency translation gains from peso strength.

Shorter-dated US government debt performed well as short interest rates moved lower following widely expected 25 basis point reductions in the federal funds rate in both October and December. Markets are pricing in one to two additional 25 basis point cuts in 2026.

The leading detractors in the quarter were BAE Systems plc (BAESF), Douglas Emmett, Inc (DEI), and Texas Instruments Incorporated (TXN). Fixed income detractors included Oracle Corporation 5.95%, due 26-Sep-2055, Mellon Capital Iv 5.1313%, due 30-Jun-2043, and Government of Brazil 10.0%, due 01-Jan-2027

BAE Systems, the largest defense contractor in the UK, traded lower following several quarters of strong performance. The company is well positioned to benefit from prospectively higher defense spending throughout Europe in the face of uncertain military support from the US, a commitment to which was underscored by the recent agreement among NATO members to increase their defense spending. BAE's long-term government contracts generate recurring revenues that underpin strong backlogs and cyclical resilience.

Douglas Emmett is a real estate investment trust with prime commercial and residential properties concentrated in premium, supply-constrained Los Angeles and Honolulu submarkets, both of which have high barriers to entry. The office leasing environment in Los Angeles is still experiencing higher vacancy rates compared to before the pandemic, and political headlines about California and potential consolidation in the entertainment industry have also weighed on shares. We note that Douglas Emmett's premium office properties have a higher occupancy rate than the overall Los Angeles office market and its residential and Honolulu properties are performing well. We believe that Los Angeles is still fundamentally a highly attractive place to live and work, and rents and occupancy should improve cyclically in time.

Texas Instruments (TI) designs, manufactures and sells analog and embedded processing chips for vital functions in a broad array of applications. Management dampened expectations for the automotive and industrial markets during the quarter. That said, TI is well positioned for eventual cyclical recovery, in our view, driven by low customer inventories, continued reshoring and anticipated increase in its capacity. Management remains committed to maximizing long-term shareholder value, including dividends and share buybacks.

Oracle (ORCL) is one of the world's largest independent enterprise software companies, and the stock traded down in the fourth quarter after a very strong rally through September. Oracle has been aggressively building out its AI infrastructure, and its capital-intensive strategy has required the company to take on significant debt. Though it has a robust backlog with customers like Meta (META) and Nvidia (NVDA), markets appear concerned about the ultimate profitability of its data-center spending. Moreover, there was speculation that Google's Gemini 3 launch gave it the lead in AI search over OpenAI (OPENAI), with which Oracle has a strategic infrastructure partnership. Data center capacity is fungible, and we continue to believe Oracle should be positioned to capture recurring and sustainable revenue streams by providing services that support the use and adoption of AI.

Bank of New York Mellon (BK) is the world's largest custody bank, with a $2.1 trillion investment management business. Despite strong earnings during the quarter, long-dated bonds—including those cited above—were pressured by a steeper yield curve and broad macro-economic concerns. Fundamentals for Mellon remain sound as strong client acquisition and higher account balances underpin investment service fees. Management's ongoing focus on expense control has contributed to wider margins to better support debt obligations, and maturing debt securities have been reinvested at higher yields.

Brazil's shorter-duration sovereign bonds suffered during the quarter on heightened political concerns leading up to its presidential election in October 2026. In December, Flavio Bolsonaro (son of former president Jair Bolsonaro, now incarcerated) announced he would run for president potentially undermining the candidacy of a more market-friendly centrist candidate against President Lula—the standard bearer of Brazil's left, who will seek a fourth term. Volatility in Brazilian assets is likely to be elevated in the run up to the election.

However, yields in Brazil remain attractive relative to other emerging and developed markets, and the currency is undervalued.

We appreciate your confidence and thank you for your support.
